papers.title: On the origin of the hypervelocity runaway star HD271791

abstract.text: We discuss the origin of the runaway early B-type star HD271791 and show that its extremely high velocity (\\\\simeq 530-920 km/s) cannot be explained within the framework of the binary-supernova ejection scenario. Instead, we suggest that HD271791 attained its peculiar velocity in the course of a strong dynamical encounter between two hard massive binaries or via an exchange encounter between a hard massive binary and a very massive star, formed through runaway mergers of ordinary massive stars in the dense core of a young massive star cluster.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper challenges the binary-supernova ejection scenario as the origin of the hypervelocity runaway star HD 271791, arguing that its velocity exceeds what can be achieved via symmetric SN disruption in a tight binary. Instead, it proposes that HD 271791 acquired its extreme velocity (530–920 km/s) through a dynamical encounter—either between two hard massive binaries or via an exchange interaction between a hard binary and a very massive star formed by runaway mergers in a dense young star cluster core.

source_fields.title: On the origin of the hypervelocity runaway star HD271791

source_fields.venue: Proceedings of the International Astronomical Union
source_fields.journal_name: Proceedings of the International Astronomical Union
source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of evaluating and refining the hypothesis that HD 271791 acquired its hypervelocity through the disintegration of a massive binary system following a supernova explosion, thereby addressing the problem of identifying a plausible dynamical origin for this high-velocity runaway star outside of Galactic Centre ejection scenarios. Dynamical ejection scenario: The dynamical ejection scenario explains the origin of high-velocity runaway stars through binary-binary encounters or close interactions between massive binaries and very massive stars, with both channels capable of producing runaways with velocities up to several hundred km s⁻¹, consistent with observations of stars like HD 271791. Binary-supernova ejection scenario: The binary-supernova ejection scenario produces runaway stars through symmetric supernova explosions in tight binary systems, where the secondary star gains high peculiar velocities—up to ~500 km s⁻¹—due to orbital dynamics, though stellar evolution limits pre-SN masses and thus the achievable speeds. HD 271791: former secondary in a massive binary system: HD 271791 is a former secondary star in a massive tight binary system that was ejected after the primary star underwent an asymmetric supernova explosion, leading to its α-element enrichment, subsolar iron abundance, and high space velocity.

abstract: a strong dynamical encounter between two hard massive binaries or via an exchange encounter between a hard massive binary and a very massive star, formed through runaway mergers of ordinary massive stars
title: On the origin of the hypervelocity runaway star HD271791

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper focuses on explaining the origin of the specific observed star HD271791, which has measured high velocity (530-920 km/s). Title and abstract indicate analysis of real observational data for this individual object.
